title: Today's Tech Brief: Troops' phones gave away location data to foreign adversaries
summary: A look at how troops' phones leaked location data and LLMs' struggles with false statements
imageAlt: Image from source article for Troops’ phones gave away location data to foreign adversaries
imageCaption: Troops’ phones gave away location data to foreign adversaries
imageSourceTitle: Troops’ phones gave away location data to foreign adversaries

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your tech brief. First up, we have a story that's got lawmakers pushing for tighter smartphone controls in the Department of Defense. It turns out that troops' phones were giving away their location data to foreign adversaries. This happened because commercial tracking data was exploited, allowing these adversaries to pinpoint the troops' locations. It's a bit like having a GPS tracker on your phone, but instead of helping you find your way, it's helping someone else find you. I mean, who needs a stalker when you've got a smartphone, right?

Next, let's talk about Large Language Models, or LLMs. If you tell an 8-year-old a lie, then immediately tell them you were just kidding, they might still remember the lie. It turns out that LLMs have a similar problem. Even when explicitly warned that a statement is false, they can still believe it. This raises some interesting questions about how we design and interact with these models. Are they learning from us, or are we just teaching them to be a bit... stubborn?

I'm also keeping an eye on a third development: the ongoing conversation about data privacy and security in the age of AI. As we learn more about how these models work and what they can do, we're also realizing just how vulnerable our data can be. It's a complex issue, but it's clear that we need to be thinking carefully about how we balance innovation with protection.

My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.

LLMs believe false statements even after explicit warnings that they're false
